title: Partition, Prompt, Aggregate: Statistical Self-Consistency in Language Models

central_claim.text: Across the paper's survey-based and synthetic tests, language-model estimates often fail to compose according to the law of total probability, and ACS population estimates reconstructed from subgroup prompts are often better aligned than direct population-level estimates.

tldr: If an LLM behaves like a conditional probability estimator, its direct population estimate should match the weighted sum of its estimates for any valid partition of that population.
The paper builds binary conditioning trees, prompts the model at every node, and tests both aggregation across splits and invariance to the order of equivalent conditions.
On the ACS income task, reconstructed population estimates are often closer to survey data than direct prompts, although the benefit is not monotonic as partitions become finer.
Self-consistency is a useful reference-free diagnostic, but it is necessary rather than sufficient for accuracy and does not establish that the model contains a correct internal distribution.

blocks.question: Why test language models with the law of total probability?
paragraphs: Many uses of in-context learning treat a prompt as a condition and the model's answer as an estimate of the corresponding conditional distribution. That interpretation carries a basic requirement: estimates for a population and for a complete partition of that population must agree after weighting the subgroup estimates by their population shares.

A model can give locally plausible answers while violating this requirement. Two statistically equivalent prompts can then produce incompatible estimates, so conclusions may depend on an arbitrary choice of prompt granularity or condition order.

blocks.question: What does this framework add to ordinary accuracy evaluation?
paragraphs: The framework separates alignment from self-consistency. Alignment asks whether an estimate matches external reference data. Self-consistency asks whether the model's own estimates obey probability identities, so it can be measured even when a trustworthy target distribution is unavailable.

The paper turns this idea into split-consistency and order-consistency scores. It also identifies the macro fallacy: in the ACS study, direct population prompting is often less accurate than explicitly eliciting and recombining finer subgroup estimates.

blocks.question: How does a binary conditioning tree expose inconsistent estimates?
paragraphs: Start with a base population at the root. Each binary attribute splits every node at a level into two non-overlapping children, so all nodes at that level remain a complete partition of the root population. The model receives a verbal description of each node's subgroup and estimates the target quantity for that subgroup.

For each level, the method also elicits subgroup population shares, normalizes them, and calculates their weighted sum. In the ACS prior protocol, each level's prior distribution is elicited 50 times and the normalized responses are averaged. A coherent estimator should give the same aggregate at every level.

Split consistency checks a node against the weighted sum of its immediate children. Order consistency asks whether the same subgroup receives the same estimate when its conditions are verbalized in a different order.

blocks.question: What does one population reconstruction look like?
paragraphs: Consider the probability that a person in the United States earns above a chosen threshold. One prompt asks for the probability over the whole population. A first tree level divides the population into people aged 31 to 68 and everyone else. A second level divides both groups by employment status.

The model estimates the income probability and population share for each subgroup. The explainer then multiplies each subgroup probability by its share and adds the products. That reconstruction and the direct root estimate refer to the same population quantity; disagreement is a self-consistency failure. Comparing each estimate with the ACS reference adds a separate alignment test.

blocks.question: What evidence supports the paper's conclusions?
paragraphs: In the ACS income experiment, Figure 3 reports that reconstructed aggregate estimates generally reduce alignment error relative to direct prompting. The average gain is positive at deeper levels but eventually declines, which is consistent with a trade-off: subgroup conditionals initially improve while increasingly specific population shares become harder to estimate.

The reference-free checks also reveal failures. In the two-attribute ACS tasks, the reported split-consistency scores at tolerance 0.02 range from 0 to 0.33 across the four displayed frontier models. On five World Values Survey questions in Canada and Indonesia, no model is uniformly consistent across questions and countries. Synthetic tennis and fantasy tasks show that the checks still operate where no external reference distribution is defined.
A one-prompt micro-to-macro instruction often improves ACS estimates, but its effect is less systematic and more model-dependent than explicit aggregation.

blocks.question: Where should these findings not be generalized?
paragraphs: The macro-fallacy alignment analysis relies primarily on ACS data. Its magnitude depends on the chosen demographic splits, probability wording, and post-hoc normalization. Finer partitions are not guaranteed to help because small, numerous subgroup priors become harder to estimate.

Self-consistency is only a necessary condition for faithful conditional inference. A model can be internally coherent and still wrong about the target distribution. The synthetic forecasting examples establish inconsistency, not real-world forecasting accuracy, and the experiments do not show that optimizing the proposed scores would improve alignment or downstream decisions.

blocks.question: What is the strongest conclusion a careful reader should keep?
paragraphs: The strongest contribution is a simple, reference-free test of whether conditional estimates compose. It exposes a failure that ordinary benchmark accuracy can miss and makes prompt granularity a measurable property rather than an informal concern.

The macro fallacy is more bounded: it is a repeated empirical pattern in the ACS analysis, not a universal rule that decomposition always improves an answer. The authors interpret the gains as evidence that useful subgroup information is more reliably elicited through decomposition, but the experiments do not directly reveal what distributions the models internally represent.

claims.text: Across the evaluated ACS, WVS, and synthetic tasks, language-model estimates frequently violate partition-based statistical self-consistency checks.
Each level of a valid binary conditioning tree forms a mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive partition of the base population.
The method reconstructs a population estimate by weighting subgroup conditional estimates by their elicited subgroup priors and summing them.
For each ACS tree level, the subgroup prior is estimated from 50 model responses after each response is normalized.
In the ACS income experiments, aggregates reconstructed from subgroup estimates are often better aligned with survey data than direct population-level estimates.
Increasing subgroup specificity initially improves conditional alignment while making subgroup priors harder to estimate, so aggregation gains are not monotonic with depth.
Micro-to-macro prompting often recovers part of the ACS aggregation benefit, but less systematically than explicit tree-based aggregation.
At tolerance 0.02, the ACS split-consistency scores shown in Table 1 range from 0 to 0.33 across the four displayed models and two prediction targets.
In the WVS evaluation, no reported model is uniformly self-consistent across the selected questions, countries, and checks.
The authors interpret the subgroup gains as suggesting that models contain useful subgroup-level information that direct aggregate prompts do not reliably elicit.
The evidence does not establish that the macro fallacy holds across arbitrary domains or that improving self-consistency will improve real-world accuracy.

visuals.title: Direct and weighted routes estimate one population target
visuals.question: Why should a macro prompt agree with a complete subgroup reconstruction?

items.label: Base population
items.detail: Both routes refer to the same target conditional distribution.

items.label: Direct macro estimate
items.detail: One prompt asks for the population-level probability p(root).

items.label: Complete subgroup partition
items.detail: Disjoint subgroup prompts cover the full population.

items.label: Subgroup probabilities
items.detail: The model estimates the target quantity within every subgroup.

items.label: Normalized population shares
items.detail: Each subgroup estimate receives its population weight.

items.label: Weighted reconstruction
items.detail: Compute Σg w_g p_g across the complete partition.

items.label: Consistency comparison
items.detail: Compare the direct estimate with the weighted reconstruction because both claim the same population target.

visuals.interpretation: A direct population prompt and a normalized weighted subgroup reconstruction are two routes to one quantity.
limitations: Agreement is necessary for self-consistency but does not establish that either estimate matches reality.
visuals.alt_text: Direct and weighted routes estimate one population target. Base population; Direct macro estimate; Complete subgroup partition; Subgroup probabilities; Normalized population shares; Weighted reconstruction: Σ w_g p_g; Consistency comparison: p(root) ?= Σ w_g p_g.
visuals.fallback: A direct population prompt and a normalized weighted subgroup reconstruction are two routes to one quantity. Base population; Direct macro estimate; Complete subgroup partition; Subgroup probabilities; Normalized population shares; Weighted reconstruction: Σ w_g p_g; Consistency comparison: p(root) ?= Σ w_g p_g.

visuals.title: Alignment and two consistency tests ask different questions
visuals.question: Which reference or invariance does each check use?

items.label: External alignment
items.detail: Compare an estimate with a trusted reference such as ACS when one exists.

items.label: Split consistency
items.detail: Compare a node's direct estimate with the prior-weighted aggregate of its immediate children.

items.label: Order consistency
items.detail: Compare the same subgroup estimate when equivalent conditions are verbalized in another order.

items.label: Macro fallacy
items.detail: In the ACS study, direct population prompting is often less accurate than explicit subgroup elicitation and recombination.

visuals.interpretation: Alignment measures accuracy against data, while split and order consistency test distinct internal probability requirements.
limitations: Passing either consistency check does not imply external accuracy, and the ACS macro-fallacy pattern is not universal.
visuals.alt_text: Alignment and two consistency tests ask different questions. External alignment; Split consistency; Order consistency; Macro fallacy.
visuals.fallback: Alignment measures accuracy against data, while split and order consistency test distinct internal probability requirements. External alignment; Split consistency; Order consistency; Macro fallacy.

visuals.title: Every complete level reconstructs the root
visuals.question: How does an exhaustive binary hierarchy return to one weighted estimate?

items.label: Root population
items.detail: A direct prompt estimates p(root) for the complete base population.

items.label: Attribute A
items.detail: One branch of the first mutually exclusive binary split.

items.label: Not attribute A
items.detail: The complementary branch completes the first partition.

items.label: A ∩ B
items.detail: A second binary attribute refines the first branch.

items.label: A ∩ not B
items.detail: Complementary refinement under the first branch.

items.label: not A ∩ B
items.detail: Second-level refinement under the complement branch.

items.label: not A ∩ not B
items.detail: Complementary refinement completes depth two.

items.label: Normalized weighted merge
items.detail: At each level, elicit population shares 50 times in the ACS prior protocol, normalize and average them, then compute Σg w_g p_g and compare with p(root).

visuals.interpretation: The hierarchy is exhaustive at every depth, and normalized subgroup shares weight every leaf back into the same root quantity.
limitations: The symbolic tree does not invent subgroup estimates or ACS values; self-consistency remains distinct from accuracy.
visuals.alt_text: Every complete level reconstructs the root. Root population; Attribute A; Not attribute A; A ∩ B; A ∩ not B; not A ∩ B; not A ∩ not B; Normalized weighted merge: 50 prior elicitations · Σ w_g p_g.
visuals.fallback: The hierarchy is exhaustive at every depth, and normalized subgroup shares weight every leaf back into the same root quantity. Root population; Attribute A; Not attribute A; A ∩ B; A ∩ not B; not A ∩ B; not A ∩ not B; Normalized weighted merge: 50 prior elicitations · Σ w_g p_g.

visuals.title: Two consistency checks preserve different invariances
visuals.question: What changes in each comparison, and what must stay equal?

items.label: Parent estimate
items.detail: Direct estimate for one node.

items.label: Immediate-child reconstruction
items.detail: Prior-weighted sum of that node's two children must match the parent.

items.label: Condition order A then B
items.detail: One verbal description of a fixed subgroup.

items.label: Condition order B then A
items.detail: An equivalent ordering must preserve the subgroup estimate.

visuals.interpretation: Split consistency tests probabilistic composition; order consistency tests invariance to equivalent condition ordering.
limitations: This figure explains the cited paper's reported mechanism or evidence; it does not extend the claim beyond the stated conditions.
visuals.alt_text: Two consistency checks preserve different invariances. Parent estimate; Immediate-child reconstruction: p(parent) ?= Σchildren w p; Condition order A then B; Condition order B then A: p(A,B) ?= p(B,A).
visuals.fallback: Split consistency tests probabilistic composition; order consistency tests invariance to equivalent condition ordering. Parent estimate; Immediate-child reconstruction: p(parent) ?= Σchildren w p; Condition order A then B; Condition order B then A: p(A,B) ?= p(B,A).

visuals.title: The ACS example reconstructs income probability from age and employment leaves
visuals.question: How do named subgroups produce a symbolic population estimate?

items.label: United States population
items.detail: Direct prompt asks for the probability of earning above the selected threshold.

items.label: Age 31–68
items.detail: First named age branch.

items.label: Age outside 31–68
items.detail: Complement branch makes the age split exhaustive.

items.label: 31–68 and employed
items.detail: Employment refines the first age group.

items.label: 31–68 and not employed
items.detail: Complementary employment leaf.

items.label: Outside 31–68 and employed
items.detail: Employment refines the complementary age group.

items.label: Outside 31–68 and not employed
items.detail: Final complement completes the four-leaf partition.

items.label: Symbolic weighted reconstruction
items.detail: For every leaf g, multiply income probability p_g by normalized share w_g, sum Σg w_g p_g, compare with the root for consistency, then compare each route with ACS separately for alignment.

visuals.interpretation: The example keeps the age complement, all employment leaves, the weighted formula, and the separate consistency/alignment comparisons visible.
limitations: The figure is symbolic and does not invent population shares, income probabilities, or an ACS outcome.
visuals.alt_text: The ACS example reconstructs income probability from age and employment leaves. United States population; Age 31–68; Age outside 31–68; 31–68 and employed; 31–68 and not employed; Outside 31–68 and employed; Outside 31–68 and not employed; Symbolic weighted reconstruction: Σg w_g p_g.
visuals.fallback: The example keeps the age complement, all employment leaves, the weighted formula, and the separate consistency/alignment comparisons visible. United States population; Age 31–68; Age outside 31–68; 31–68 and employed; 31–68 and not employed; Outside 31–68 and employed; Outside 31–68 and not employed; Symbolic weighted reconstruction: Σg w_g p_g.

visuals.title: Deeper reconstruction helps, then its average gain declines
visuals.question: What pattern is reported, and what conclusion remains unestablished?

items.label: Direct population prompt
items.detail: Baseline alignment error in the ACS income analysis.

items.label: Reconstructed aggregate
items.detail: Generally reduces alignment error relative to direct prompting.

items.label: Early depth gain
items.detail: Average reconstruction gain is positive as subgroup conditionals initially improve.

items.label: Later decline
items.detail: The average gain eventually falls as increasingly specific subgroup shares become harder to estimate.

items.label: No monotonic guarantee
items.detail: The source does not establish that every finer partition improves alignment.

visuals.interpretation: The evidence supports a qualitative depth trade-off, not a monotonic decomposition rule.
limitations: No unreported coordinates are inferred from Figure 3, and the pattern is primarily an ACS finding.
visuals.alt_text: Deeper reconstruction helps, then its average gain declines. Direct population prompt; Reconstructed aggregate; Early depth gain; Later decline; No monotonic guarantee.
visuals.fallback: The evidence supports a qualitative depth trade-off, not a monotonic decomposition rule. Direct population prompt; Reconstructed aggregate; Early depth gain; Later decline; No monotonic guarantee.

visuals.title: Reference-free failures appear across three task families
visuals.question: Which values and conclusions belong to each evaluation?

items.label: Displayed frontier models
items.detail: Two-attribute ACS tasks include four displayed models.

items.label: Split-consistency range
items.detail: Reported scores across the displayed models and two prediction targets.

items.label: World Values Survey questions
items.detail: Five questions are evaluated in Canada and Indonesia.

items.label: Uniform consistency
items.detail: No reported model is uniformly consistent across the selected questions, countries, and checks.

items.label: Tennis forecasting
items.detail: Reference-free checks expose inconsistency without an external target distribution.

items.label: Fantasy forecasting
items.detail: A second synthetic family demonstrates the same reference-free operation.

items.label: Forecasting boundary
items.detail: Detecting inconsistency does not establish real-world forecasting accuracy.

visuals.interpretation: ACS supplies a disclosed tolerance and score range; WVS and synthetic tasks establish different qualitative consistency findings.
limitations: The task-family panels are not one scale and do not reproduce unreported per-model values.

glossary.definition: A recursive set of binary splits whose nodes at every depth form a partition of the base population.
A population-level estimate produced by weighting and summing subgroup conditional estimates.
The paper's name for the observed pattern in which direct population prompting is less aligned than explicit subgroup elicitation and aggregation.
Agreement between a direct estimate for one node and the prior-weighted aggregate of estimates for its child partition.
Invariance of an estimate when equivalent conditioning constraints are verbalized in different orders.

revision_notes: Initial explainer draft based on arXiv:2607.15277v1.
Kept the macro-fallacy claim scoped to the ACS alignment experiments.
Marked broader accuracy and internal-knowledge conclusions as not established.
Uses an original static mechanism diagram because the source license is CC BY-NC-ND 4.0.
Visual revision 3: paragraph-scoped figures now encode the manifest-selected topology, conditions, values, and evidence boundaries.
